title: Stolen Credibility
description: A strategic look at AI-generated financial commentary and deepfake authority. As the cost of producing convincing expert content collapses, the credibility of institutions and public figures becomes easier to exploit.
file-link: blogs/Substack/Stolen Credibility - by Richard Rumelt.pdf

title: The Amelia Paradox
description: An examination of how digital audiences can repurpose institutional narratives. A character designed to discourage extremist views is captured by online communities and turned into a viral meme, illustrating how control of meaning is lost in networked media.
file-link: blogs/Substack/The Amelia Paradox - by Richard Rumelt.pdf

title: Tesla's Honorable Discharge
description: A strategic interpretation of Tesla's quiet retreat from the Model S and Model X. The essay explores how companies sometimes "honorably discharge" legacy products that no longer fit the economic logic of the firm.
file-link: blogs/Substack/Tesla's Honorable Discharge - by Richard Rumelt.pdf

title: The Shade of Strategy
description: A reflection on Bruce Henderson's early essays and the origins of modern strategy thinking. Introduces the idea of short analytical "perspectives" as a way to clarify problems rather than produce plans.
file-link: blogs/Substack/The Shade of Strategy - by Richard Rumelt.pdf

title: The New Name: Rumelt Perspectives
description: An introduction to the publication's new title and its focus on strategy as diagnosis and problem solving rather than aspiration and planning.
file-link: blogs/Substack/The New Name_ Rumelt Perspectives - by Richard Rumelt.pdf

title: Can California Build High-Speed Rail?
description: A diagnosis of California's troubled high-speed rail project. The essay highlights the institutional and organizational barriers that often derail large infrastructure initiatives.
file-link: blogs/Substack/Can California Build High-Speed Rail_ - by Richard Rumelt.pdf

title: Becoming a Strategist (Part 3)
description: The final essay in a three-part series on learning the craft of strategy. Focuses on identifying the crux of a problem and applying concentrated force at that point.
file-link: blogs/Substack/Becoming a Strategist (Part 3) - by Richard Rumelt.pdf

title: Becoming a Strategist (Part 2)
description: Examines the development of strategic judgment and the role of disciplined thinking and feedback in improving strategic capability.
file-link: blogs/Substack/Becoming a Strategist (Part 2 corrected).pdf

title: Becoming a Strategist (Part 1)
description: Introduces the strategist's mindset: focusing on problems, recognizing constraints, and avoiding the trap of mistaking goals for strategy.
file-link: blogs/Substack/Becoming a Strategist (Part 1) - by Richard Rumelt.pdf

title: A Strategist's Basic Bookshelf
description: A curated reading list of books that illuminate the practice of strategy across business, history, and leadership.
file-link: blogs/Substack/A Strategist's Basic Bookshelf - by Richard Rumelt.pdf

title: The U.S. Needs an Office of Competitive Assessment
description: A proposal for a national institution dedicated to diagnosing strategic competition and long-term geopolitical challenges.
file-link: blogs/Substack/The U.S. Needs an Office of Competitive Assessment.pdf

title: Intel's Fall From Grace
description: An analysis of Intel's strategic decline and the organizational commitments that limited its ability to adapt to technological change.
file-link: blogs/Substack/Intel's Fall From Grace - by Richard Rumelt.pdf

title: The Standard Narrative
description: A critique of the typical corporate strategy document, which often substitutes aspirations and narratives for real strategic choices.
file-link: blogs/Substack/The Standard Narrative - by Richard Rumelt.pdf

title: The Problem of Development
description: An exploration of why many developing economies fail to generate sustained growth, focusing on institutional incentives and misallocation of talent.
file-link: blogs/Substack/The Problem of Development - by Richard Rumelt.pdf

title: The Logic of the Chain
description: Explores chain-link systems where performance is limited by the weakest element, and why incremental improvements can sometimes reduce overall effectiveness.
file-link: blogs/Substack/The Logic of The Chain - by Richard Rumelt.pdf

title: The Dark Side of Business
description: Examines strategies that extract value rather than create it, and the broader social costs of competitive behavior that exploits rather than serves customers.
file-link: blogs/Substack/The Dark Side of Business - by Richard Rumelt.pdf

title: The Sound of a Domino Falling
description: On strategic inflection points and how to recognize when a competitive position is beginning to unravel.
file-link: blogs/Substack/The Sound of a Domino Falling - by Richard Rumelt.pdf

title: Strategy at an Inflection Point
description: How major shifts in technology, markets, or regulation create both threats and opportunities for strategic repositioning.
file-link: blogs/Substack/Strategy at an Inflection Point - by Richard Rumelt.pdf

title: Richard Helms Explains Skimming
description: A case study in how intelligence organizations and other institutions can be captured by internal interests that prioritize self-preservation over mission.
file-link: blogs/Substack/Richard Helms Explains Skimming - by Richard Rumelt.pdf

title: Is Management About People?
description: Questions the popular view that management is primarily about motivating and developing people, arguing that strategic clarity about the work itself is the more fundamental challenge.
file-link: blogs/Substack/Is Management About People_ - by Richard Rumelt.pdf

title: Distributed Knowledge
description: On the competitive advantage that comes from knowledge that is embedded in organizational routines and relationships, and therefore difficult to imitate.
file-link: blogs/Substack/Distributed Knowledge - by Richard Rumelt.pdf

title: Blockbuster
description: A strategic post-mortem on Blockbuster's failure to adapt to digital distribution, examining the internal logic that made change so difficult.
file-link: blogs/Substack/Blockbuster - by Richard Rumelt - The Rumelt Perspectives.pdf
